explain.rule: Comparatives compare two things. Superlatives describe the extreme in a group. Short adjectives (1 syllable): add -er/-est. Long adjectives (3+ syllables): use more/most. Two-syllable adjectives: usually more/most, but adjectives ending in -y, -er, -ow, -le often take -er/-est. Irregulars: good→better→best, bad→worse→worst, far→farther→farthest, many/much→more→most, little→less→least. Use 'than' after comparatives. Use 'the' before superlatives. NEVER combine: never say 'more taller' or 'the most best'.
explain.why_matters: Comparatives and superlatives appear in almost every conversation involving choices, opinions, descriptions, and decisions. Essential for everyday English.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew uses יותר for all comparatives and הכי for all superlatives — always the same pattern regardless of adjective length. In English you must choose: -er or more? The most common mistake is 'more tall' instead of 'taller'. Also remember: irregular forms (good/better/best) must be memorised — there is no equivalent of 'gooder' or 'most good'.
explain.tip: Count syllables: 1 syllable → -er/-est always (tall→taller, big→bigger). 3+ syllables → more/most always (expensive→more expensive). 2 syllables: mostly more/most is safe, but -y endings use -er/-est (easy→easier, heavy→heavier). Irregulars must be memorised. Test yourself: can you put 'more' in front of it? good → more good? NO → best. tall → more tall? NO → taller.

spelling.rule: 1 syllable CVC: double final consonant + er/est
Ending in consonant+y: change y to i + er/est

good_examples.sentence: This bag is heavier than that one.
good_examples.audio_text: This bag is heavier than that one.

good_examples.sentence: She is more experienced than the other candidates.
good_examples.audio_text: She is more experienced than the other candidates.

good_examples.sentence: This is the best coffee I've ever had.
good_examples.audio_text: This is the best coffee I've ever had.

good_examples.sentence: The north is colder than the south.
good_examples.audio_text: The north is colder than the south.

good_examples.sentence: It's the most beautiful city I've visited.
good_examples.audio_text: It's the most beautiful city I've visited.

good_examples.sentence: The situation is worse than we expected.
good_examples.audio_text: The situation is worse than we expected.

good_examples.sentence: This hotel is not as expensive as the one in the centre.
good_examples.audio_text: This hotel is not as expensive as the one in the centre.

good_examples.sentence: The more you practise, the better you get.
good_examples.audio_text: The more you practise, the better you get.
good_examples.note: Double comparative: the more... the better

good_examples.sentence: He runs faster than anyone else on the team.
good_examples.audio_text: He runs faster than anyone else on the team.

good_examples.sentence: She works less efficiently under pressure.
good_examples.audio_text: She works less efficiently under pressure.

bad_examples.wrong: She is more tall than her sister.
bad_examples.correct: She is taller than her sister.
bad_examples.reason: tall = 1 syllable → use -er. Never combine more + -er.

bad_examples.wrong: This is the most good solution.
bad_examples.reason: good is irregular → best. Never 'most good'.

bad_examples.wrong: He is more better at maths.
bad_examples.reason: better is already the comparative of good. Never add more to an irregular comparative.

bad_examples.correct: This is the most expensive hotel.
bad_examples.reason: expensive = 3 syllables → the most expensive. Never add -est to long adjectives.

bad_examples.wrong: She is taller as her brother.
bad_examples.correct: She is taller than her brother.
bad_examples.reason: Use 'than' after comparatives. 'As...as' is for equal comparisons only.

exercises.question: This bag is ___ (heavy) than that one.
exercises.hint: heavy ends in y → change y to i + er
exercises.hebrew_note: תואר שמסתיים ב-y: שנה ל-i + er

exercises.question: This is the ___ (good) pizza I have ever tasted.
The new office is ___ (modern) than the old one.
This is the ___ (bad) traffic I have ever seen.
It was the ___ (hot) day of the year.
exercises.hint: hot = 1 syllable, CVC → double t + est
exercises.explanation: hot → hottest. CVC rule: double the final consonant.

exercises.question: He is not ___ (tall) ___ his father.
exercises.explanation: not as tall as = he is shorter than his father.

exercises.question: Which country is ___ (far) from Israel — Australia or Brazil?
The ___ you practise, the ___ (good) you become.
exercises.hint: Double comparative: the more..., the + comparative
exercises.explanation: The more..., the better — double comparative structure.

exercises.question: She is ___ (experienced) than the other candidates.
This is ___ (expensive) option but also ___ (reliable).
exercises.answer: the most expensive / the most reliable
exercises.hint: Both are superlatives of long adjectives
exercises.explanation: expensive, reliable → the most expensive, the most reliable.

exercises.question: She is ___ (old) of the three sisters.
exercises.explanation: old → oldest. The oldest of the three.

exercises.question: This coffee is ___ (strong) than the one I had this morning.
This exercise is ___ than the last one.
She is ___ player on the team.
His new flat is ___ than his old one.
exercises.hint: big = 1 syllable, CVC → double g + er
exercises.explanation: big → bigger. CVC: double final consonant + er.

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.answer: This film is worse than I expected.
exercises.explanation: bad → worse → worst. Worse than = comparative.
distractors: This film is more bad than I expected.
This film is badder than I expected.
This film is more worse than I expected.

exercises.question: He's not ___ his brother.
exercises.explanation: not as tall as = he is shorter.

exercises.question: She speaks English ___ anyone else in the class.
Find and fix: 'She is more tall than her sister.'
exercises.answer: She is taller than her sister.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'This is the most good restaurant in town.'
exercises.answer: This is the best restaurant in town.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'He is more better at chess than me.'
exercises.answer: He is better at chess than me.
exercises.hint: better is already a comparative — can you add more?
exercises.explanation: better = comparative of good. Never add more to irregular comparatives.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'This is the expensivest hotel.'
exercises.answer: This is the most expensive hotel.
exercises.explanation: Long adjectives → the most. Never -est with long adjectives.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'She is taller as her mother.'
exercises.answer: She is taller than her mother.
exercises.explanation: Use 'than' after comparatives. As...as = equal comparisons only.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: This is the most expensive hotel in the city.
exercises.hint: Superlative of expensive: the most expensive

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The sequel is not as good as the original film.
exercises.explanation: not as good as = expressing inferiority.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The more you practise, the better you get.
exercises.hint: Double comparative: The more..., the better...

speak.sentence: This is the most interesting book I've read in years.
chunks: This is the most interesting book

speak.sentence: She's much better at maths than I am.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Better' = BET-er. 'Maths' = MATHS — don't forget the final S (British English).

speak.sentence: The weather here is not as hot as it is at home in the summer.
chunks: The weather here is not as hot
as it is at home in the summer.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Weather' = WETH-er — th sound between teeth. 'Summer' = SUM-er.

speak.sentence: The situation is getting worse and worse every day.
chunks: The situation is getting worse and worse

speak.sentence: Which is the cheapest way to get to the airport from here?
chunks: to get to the airport from here?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Cheapest' = CHEEP-ist. 'Airport' = AIR-port — stress on first syllable.

speak.sentence: He's more experienced than the other applicants.
The more you sleep, the better you feel.
This is the worst traffic I've seen in years.
